text: So I do about five hundred and fifty bariatric surgeries a year. About twenty percent of those are revisions. So I do over three hundred sleeves a year. The number one tip and technique is if you're not comfortable, no one is. So you've got to set up the OR expressly to your needs.

The preparation for surgery, the team getting them ready for surgery, the things we do to get people ready are above and beyond, well beyond COE needs and expectations, but it's my comfort level for their safety. Once we're in the OR, every person in there is not trying to do the right thing.

They know the right thing to do based on the standards we've created. I do my own prep, which is important. It frees up the nurse to do everything she needs to do. Karen and Richie can then count. I always go super umbilical.

The surgeon positions the OptiView port approximately a hand's breadth below the xiphoid process, closer to the umbilicus for women and higher for men, ensuring access to the hiatus. The midline fascia and peritoneum are visible. The primary port is a 12mm, with the next ancillary port placed at the right upper midline, followed by a subxiphoid port above the liver.

The surgeon inspects the anatomy, noting a normal-sized liver and absence of an anterior hiatal hernia. The pylorus is identified, and the surgeon plans to leave approximately six centimeters of the antrum. A vertical line is visualized from the lesser curvature to guide the procedure.

The surgeon decompresses the stomach and begins dissection more than halfway down the greater curvature using a harmonic scalpel, carefully staying close to the stomach to avoid the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to carefully dissect near the gastroepiploic vessels, avoiding disruption. A soft grasper is used to gently retract the spleen towards the angle of His, minimizing potential injury.

The surgeon encounters difficulty reaching the upper area due to notable vascular supply, which is somewhat hidden. The decision is made to start dissection lower on the stomach and work upwards, ensuring easier access and effective retraction of the stomach.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the stomach, flipping it upwards to expose the superior aspect where the gastropaploic artery is close to the stomach. Multiple grabs and re-grabs are performed to manage the blood supply in this critical area. The surgeon identifies a thin veil of tissue without vessels as they approach the hiatus.

The surgeon begins using an energy device to address small vasculature near the stomach, ensuring to stay off the stomach tissue. The assistant helps maintain a clear view of the surgical field, facilitating precise and methodical progress.

The surgeon observes the left cruse after taking most of the stomach down, noting the absence of a hiatal hernia. The area is now clearly visible, and a 34 French bougie is used as the stapler is turned.

The surgeon uses a green seam guard buttressing with a powered Echelon stapler 60 to staple the stomach, ensuring the cut edge is a full four centimeters from the incisura. The assistant, Tony, carefully grabs the cut edge to prevent a barbershop pole effect, while the surgeon pulls down to lengthen the stomach and holds it in place for at least fifteen seconds.

The surgeon positions the pylorus six centimeters away, waits for fifteen seconds, and pulses. The bougie is visible, and adjustments are made to prevent it from bowing out. Tony carefully grabs the cut edge of the tissue, ensuring precise alignment and preventing a barbershop pole effect.

The surgeon adjusts the angulation of the stapler using wrist movements, ensuring it maintains a safe distance while approaching the bougie. The stapler is positioned equidistantly anterior and posterior, and the surgeon wiggles the bougie to confirm its fit. After waiting approximately fifteen seconds, the surgeon pulses the stapler and prepares to switch to blue staples, with Tony assisting by holding the tissue at the cut edge.

The surgeon turns the stapler in line with the gastric crotch, ensuring the correct anterior and posterior distance. Using the Echelon-powered stapler, the surgeon pulses for fifteen seconds at the gastroesophageal junction. The assistant provides a view under the area, allowing the surgeon to grab the fundus and adjust the stapler angle with wrist movement, facilitating precise stapling with ample freedom of movement.

The surgeon adjusts the stapler slightly off the staple line, using a blue cartridge, and angles it for safe placement near the hiatus without causing injury. The bougie moves easily, indicating adequate space at the hiatus and post-hiatus area, where the cardio of the stomach is located. The area broadens slightly, providing space for fullness and potential adjustments if needed.

The surgeon evaluates the sleeve gastrectomy, noting the shape and retraction of the stomach tissue. Pulsing is applied to ensure hemostasis, with the squared-off appearance of the sleeve being emphasized.

The surgeon examines the staple line, which appears smooth and without kinks or twists. The assistant flips the tip of the staple line down, ensuring it is tapered for easier removal. The staple line shows excellent integrity with no wavering anteriorly or posteriorly.
The surgeon applies Evaseal to the staple line, enhancing the hemostatic effect and ensuring the integrity of the surgical site.
